companyCaseStudy.title: How Instagram Scaled Postgres, Memcached & Feed Fanout to 100M+ Users (From 2k to 40k+ writes/s)
companyCaseStudy.summary: Instagram grew from a few million to 100M+ users in a couple of years and hit database saturation: single Postgres instances struggled under tens of thousands of writes per second. They solved it with pragmatic sharding, aggressive caching, background fanout, and object storage—cutting feed latency by an order of magnitude and moving DB load from a single hotspot to many shards.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Instagram went from a toy app to a global social network in months. By the time they crossed ~100M users, they were dealing with order-of-magnitude increases in write and read load: sustained peak write traffic in the tens of thousands per second, millions of cache lookups per second, and photo storage exploding into hundreds of TBs. The original monolithic Postgres setup was the bottleneck.

---

## The problem

- Monolithic Django app backed by one or a small number of Postgres instances.
- Photo blobs consumed storage and throughput (migrated to S3).
- User timeline reads (home feed) were read-heavy: many users request feeds often; naive queries had complex joins and slow tail latencies.
- Writes (likes, comments, follows, uploads) created hotspots: single DB masters hit CPU, locks and connection limits.

Concrete-ish numbers observed during scaling phases (publicly discussed or broadly reported):
- User base: 10s → 100M+ users
- Peak write throughput: grew from thousands/s to tens of thousands/s
- Cache hit targets: >95% to keep DB load manageable
- Large media footprint: 100s of TBs (photos) stored on S3

### Challenges

1. How to split relational data while keeping Django code maintainable
2. Reduce tail latency for feed read (99th percentile)
3. Keep feature velocity—developers must not wrestle with shards constantly
4. Avoid duplicating too much state while keeping reads cheap

---

## The solution (story: Problem → Challenge → Solution)

### 1) Move blobs out of DB

Problem: Postgres was storing metadata + media references, and media traffic stressed storage/IO.

Solution: Serve images from S3 (or a CDN backed by S3). Store only URLs/metadata in the DB. That immediately relieved DB I/O and reduced replica lag for metadata queries.

Result: Media storage and egress scaled independently; database IOPS demand dropped significantly.

### 2) Horizontal sharding of Postgres by user_id

Problem: A single Postgres master couldn't handle peak write throughput and connection/CPU pressure.

Solution: Shard user-facing relational data by user_id (hash/mod scheme). Each shard is a distinct Postgres instance (master + replicas). A simple routing layer in Django (DATABASE_ROUTERS) routes writes/reads by the owner (user_id), keeping related rows on the same shard.

Why by user_id? Ownership-based sharding keeps most user-scoped transactions single-shard and predictable.

Tradeoffs considered:
- Range-sharding by ID sequence (good for scaling via ranges, bad for hotspots)
- Hash/mod sharding by user_id (uniform distribution, simple)
- Application-level microservices that own a domain (more refactor)

They chose the pragmatic: hash sharding + helper libraries inside the Django stack so developer ergonomics stayed high.

### 3) Aggressive caching (Memcached) with high cache-hit targets

Problem: Even with sharding, read volume for feeds, comments and profiles was enormous.

Solution: Cache warm objects aggressively in Memcached (user profile, recent media list, precomputed feed pages). Keep TTLs tuned and do cache-aside for complex objects.

Result: Cache hit rates above 95% meant replicas served far fewer read queries; DB CPU and I/O decreased.

### 4) Precompute feeds: fanout-on-write + per-user feed store (Redis / cache)

Problem: Naively generating the home feed by pulling from many followees (fanout-on-read) causes a huge read fan-in for popular users. The feed read path was expensive and high-latency.

Solution: Precompute feeds at write time (fanout-on-write). When a user posts, background workers (Celery) push that post into the per-follower feed stores (in Redis or cache-backed lists) and/or into per-user feed tables on the appropriate shards.

Architecture notes:
- Writes enqueue a fanout job to a reliable queue (e.g., RabbitMQ/Redis/beanstalk/Resque/Celery).
- Distributed Celery workers read the job and push the new feed item into each follower's feed list, stored either in Redis (for very hot users) or persisted into sharded Postgres feed tables for longer-term storage.
- Hot user optimization: large-fanout users (celebrities) are handled with hybrid strategies—partial push + on-demand pull for super-followers to avoid O(N) operations.

Result: Read latency for home-feed dropped dramatically because the read path became a O(1) read from a precomputed list. Total end-to-end feed read 99p latency went from hundreds of ms to tens of ms.

### 5) Background processing and reliable queues

Problem: Synchronous fanout would make writes blocking and fragile.

Solution: Use background workers and a durable queue to process heavy fanout tasks. Retry policies, idempotent workers, and idempotency keys prevented duplicate feed items on retries. Workers were horizontally scaled and monitored closely.

### 6) Operational: schema migrations, connect pooling, replica lag handling

- Use connection pooling (pgbouncer) to reduce per-process DB connection counts.
- Migrations run shard-by-shard with rolling deploys and scripts to avoid cross-shard downtime.
- Health checks and circuit-breakers around stale replicas (route reads to healthy replicas only).

---

## Architecture (visual)

### High level architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Mobile/Web Client] --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> App[Django App Servers]
  App --> Cache[Memcached Cluster]
  App --> ShardRouter[DB Router]
  ShardRouter --> PG1[Postgres Shard 1]
  ShardRouter --> PG2[Postgres Shard 2]
  ShardRouter --> PGN[Postgres Shard N]
  App --> S3[Object Storage (S3) & CDN]
  App --> Queue[Durable Queue]
  Queue --> Workers[Celery / Worker Fleet]
  Workers --> FeedStore[Per-User Feed Store (Redis or DB)]
  Client --> CDN
  CDN --> S3
```

### Upload & Feed flow (flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  U[User uploads photo] --> A[App writes metadata to shard]
  A --> B[Store photo in S3]
  A --> C[Enqueue fanout job]
  C --> D[Background workers]
  D --> E[Push item to followers' feed stores]
  F[Followers request feed] --> G[Read from per-user feed store / cache]
  G --> H[Return precomputed feed]
```

---

## Before vs After (impact)

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| DB topology | Single master / small set of masters | Many Postgres shards (hash by user_id) + replicas |
| Peak write throughput handled | ~2k/s (early) | Tens of thousands/s across shards |
| Feed read latency (p99) | 300-800ms | 20-80ms |
| Cache hit rate | ~70% | 95%+ |
| Photo storage cost on DB | High (heavy I/O) | Shifted to S3/CDN (massively cheaper) |

Note: numbers here are representative of public engineering descriptions and typical scale jumps Instagram reported during rapid growth phases.

---

## Results & tradeoffs

- Result: The combination of sharding, aggressive caching, and fanout-on-write allowed Instagram to keep feed read latency low at planetary scale and to continue rapid feature development on top of a familiar Django stack.
- Tradeoffs: More complex background processing, more operational shards to manage, increased storage duplication (feed items stored per follower), and handling of extreme fanout for celebrity accounts.

---

## Observability & reliability

- Monitor replication lag per shard and surface circuit-breakers for stale replicas.
- Track cache hit rate, queue backlog, worker latency, and fanout job durations.
- Chaos drills: simulate shard loss and test rapid re-routing and degraded read modes.

---

## TL;DR: Why this worked

- Keep app-level developer ergonomics (Django) while adding a small routing layer for shards.
- Move heavy media out of the DB into S3/CDN.
- Precompute expensive reads (fanout-on-write) so reads are cheap and predictable.
- Use caches and pools aggressively to keep DB load manageable.
keyTakeaways: Practical sharding by owner (user_id) keeps most transactions single-shard and predictable—use hash/mod sharding to avoid hotspots at the cost of more shards to manage.
Move large binary objects to object storage (S3 + CDN) early: it reduces DB I/O and replica lag significantly.
Precompute expensive read work (fanout-on-write) to convert variable, heavy read costs into more predictable background write costs. Handle super-fans with hybrid strategies.
Operational patterns matter: connection pooling, per-shard migrations, queue monitoring, and replica-lag-aware routing are essential to make sharding sustainable.

concept.title: How Consistent Hashing Keeps Your Shards from Exploding When Nodes Come & Go
concept.summary: Consistent hashing minimizes data reshuffling when you add or remove nodes, making sharded caches, distributed stores, and CDNs practical. It turns the painful 'reassign everything' operation into a small, predictable change so systems stay available and balanced.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

Imagine you run a distributed cache or a key-value cluster. A single node dies or you add capacity — and suddenly 100% of keys need to move. Rebalancing that much state is slow, error-prone, and can cause outages. *Consistent hashing* solves this: when the cluster membership changes, only ~K/N of keys move (K = total keys, N = nodes), not everything.

**Analogy:** Think of keys as houses distributed around a circular island and servers as mailboxes placed on the same circular road. A letter goes to the first mailbox you encounter clockwise from the house. If a mailbox disappears, only the houses that used that mailbox are affected — they simply send mail to the next mailbox clockwise.

---

## Core idea (short)

- Hash both nodes and keys to the same numeric space (e.g., 0..2^32).
- Arrange the space as a ring (wrap-around).
- A key maps to the first node clockwise from the key's hash.
- When nodes join/leave, only keys between neighboring nodes need reassignment.

### Visual: How it works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client: get("user:42")] --> HashKey[Hash("user:42")]
  HashKey --> RingPos[Find position on ring]
  RingPos -->|clockwise search| NodeC[Node C]
  NodeC --> Store[Serve or store key]
```

---

## Before / After: naive modulo vs consistent hashing

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Key % N (modulo sharding) | Simple, fast lookups | When N changes, nearly all keys move; poor for dynamic clusters | Small, static clusters with stable membership |
| Consistent Hashing | Minimal remapping on membership change; smooth scaling | Slightly more complex; needs virtual nodes & good hashing to avoid hotspots | Dynamic clusters, caches, CDNs, distributed hash tables |

---

## Implementation sketch (how to build one)

Practical additions you will want:
- *Virtual nodes (vnodes):* map each physical node to many points on the ring to smooth distribution.
- *Replication:* pick the next R distinct nodes clockwise to store replicas.
- *Sorted structure:* store ring points in a sorted array or balanced tree for O(log M) lookup (M = vnode count).

### Simple Python example (conceptual)

```python
import bisect, hashlib

class ConsistentHashRing:
    def __init__(self, nodes=None, vnodes=100):
        self.vnodes = vnodes
        self.ring = []          # sorted list of hashes
        self.node_map = {}      # hash -> node
        if nodes:
            for n in nodes:
                self.add_node(n)

    def _hash(self, key):
        return int(hashlib.md5(key.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)

    def add_node(self, node):
        for i in range(self.vnodes):
            token = f"{node}#{i}"
            h = self._hash(token)
            bisect.insort(self.ring, h)
            self.node_map[h] = node

    def remove_node(self, node):
        to_remove = [h for h, n in self.node_map.items() if n == node]
        for h in to_remove:
            self.ring.pop(bisect.bisect_left(self.ring, h))
            del self.node_map[h]

    def get_node(self, key):
        h = self._hash(key)
        idx = bisect.bisect(self.ring, h) % len(self.ring)
        return self.node_map[self.ring[idx]]
```

This shows the core: hash keys and tokens, keep a sorted list of vnode hashes, and find the first vnode ≥ key-hash (wrap if needed).

---

## When to use consistent hashing

- Distributed caches (memcached clusters) to avoid costly re-hash on scale events.
- Sharding user data / partitioning where nodes may be added/removed often.
- CDNs and DHTs (distributed hash tables) where nodes are volatile.
- Peer-to-peer systems (BitTorrent-like routing, Kademlia variants use related ideas).

## Common pitfalls & how to avoid them

- *Too few virtual nodes -> uneven load.* Fix: increase vnodes per physical node (hundreds or thousands depending on key count).
- *Bad hash function -> collisions or skew.* Fix: use a strong hash (MD5, SHA-1, or xxHash/CityHash for speed) and test distribution.
- *Hot keys still concentrate traffic even with perfect hashing.* Fix: handle very hot keys specially (cache them everywhere, use a hotspot circuit breaker, or split key namespace).
- *Replication semantics unclear.* Decide whether replication is "next R distinct physical nodes clockwise" and ensure failure handling and hinted handoff on node failure.
- *Stateful nodes & migration cost.* Even though only a fraction of keys move, moving many MB/GB per key may still be heavy — do streaming/gradual transfer and throttling.
- *Consistent hashing + small cluster sizes.* With very few nodes, distribution variance is high; vnodes help but sometimes hashing alone isn't enough.

---

## Real-world examples

- Amazon Dynamo uses consistent hashing with virtual nodes and hinted handoff for availability.
- Memcached clients (e.g., ketama) use consistent hashing to spread keys across cache servers.

---

## Quick decision guide

- Need minimal reshuffle when nodes change? -> Use consistent hashing.
- Cluster membership static and tiny? -> Simple modulo might suffice.
- Need replication and strong consistency? -> Combine consistent hashing with replication maps and a consensus layer for membership.

---

## Closing analogy (practical):

Consistent hashing is like designing a road map so that when a shop closes, only the block it served has to walk to the next shop — not every single customer in town. That small, predictable disruption is what makes large dynamic distributed systems manageable.
keyTakeaways: Consistent hashing minimizes key movement on node joins/leaves — only ~K/N keys remap instead of all keys.
Use virtual nodes to smooth distribution and replicate by taking the next R nodes clockwise.
Watch out for hotspots, choose a strong hash, and combine with streaming migration when moving large state.
